en.extract:
The lucifer sheartail or lucifer hummingbird (Calothorax lucifer) is a medium-sized, 10 cm long, green hummingbird with a slightly curved bill and distinctive outward flare of its gorget feathers. Its habitat is in high-altitude areas of northern Mexico and southwestern United States. It winters in central Mexico.


Taxonomy
The lucifer sheartail was formally described in 1827 by the English zoologist William Swainson based on a specimen collected at Temascaltepec in central Mexico. Swainson  placed the species in the genus Cynanthus and coined the binomial name Cynanthus lucifer. The specific epithet lucifer is Latin meaning "light-bearing" from lux, lucis meaning "light" and -fera meaning "-bearing". The lucifer sheartail is now placed with the beautiful sheartail in the genus Calothorax that was introduced in 1840 by the English zoologist George Robert Gray.


Description
The lucifer sheartail is a medium-sized, 10-centimetre (3.9 in) long, green hummingbird with a long curved bill, small wings, and white streak behind its eye. The male has an iridescent plumage, forked dark tail, green crown, long magenta gorget, and white underparts. The female is larger with duller plumage, pale throat and white or buff feathers underside, usually with crimson trim.


Distribution
The lucifer sheartail is distributed to deserts and arid areas with agave plants in the southwestern United States, from southwest Texas, extreme southwestern New Mexico to southeastern Arizona, and in central and northern Mexico. It is also found in the Madrean sky islands of the northern end of the Sierra Madre Occidental, Mexico. Its preferred habitat tends to be at altitudes of 3,500–5,500 feet (1,100–1,700 m) in canyons, mountain slopes, and dry washes having desert shrubs and cacti. In winter, the birds migrate to central Mexico.


Diet
The diet consists mainly of nectar from agave and colorful desert flowers, spiders and small insects. Lucifer sheartails have a typical hummingbird flight style while feeding from flowers, catching insects in flight, and flying in straight lines to specific destinations for other food, the nest or for roosting. Males defend feeding areas from males, other females, and black-chinned hummingbirds. However, most C. lucifer are non-territorial and forage at undefended agave or agave surrounded by dense vegetation.


Breeding and behavior
During courtship, males attract females by hovering high above the female, then dive with the wings or tail making a snap sound, then flying away with the tail feathers forked and making a different series of snapping sounds. The display lasts 30 to 45 seconds and may repeat several times an hour.
Females build nests on desert shrubs or cacti on steep, dry, rocky slopes, typically 2–10 feet (0.61–3.05 m) above ground, sometimes on top of a previous nest. 
The female lays two white eggs in the small cup-like nest, having one or two broods per season. The egg incubation duration is about 15 days, and the chicks nest for about 23 days. 


Status
A locally common species in its range, the lucifer sheartail is evaluated as stable and Least Concern on the IUCN Red List of Threatened Species.

References


External links

The '''lucifer sheartail''' or '''lucifer hummingbird''' ('''''Calothorax lucifer''''') is a medium-sized, 10 cm long, green [[hummingbird]] with a slightly curved [[beak|bill]] and distinctive outward flare of its [[gorget (bird)|gorget]] feathers. Its habitat is in high-[[altitude]] areas of [[northern Mexico]] and [[southwestern United States]]. It winters in central [[Mexico]].

==Taxonomy==
The lucifer sheartail was [[species description|formally described]] in 1827 by the English zoologist [[William Swainson]] based on a specimen collected at [[Temascaltepec]] in central Mexico. Swainson  placed the species in the genus ''[[Cynanthus]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Cynanthus lucifer''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Swainson | first=William | author-link=William Swainson | year=1827 | title=A synopsis of the birds discovered in Mexico by W. Bullock, F.L.S. and Mr. William Bullock jun. | journal=Philosophical Magazine | series=New Series | volume=1 | pages=364–369, 433–442 [442] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/15530452 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1945 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=5 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=144 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/50583 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''lucifer'' is [[Latin]] meaning "light-bearing" from ''lux'', ''lucis'' meaning "light" and ''-fera'' meaning "-bearing".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=lucifer | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=lucifer | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=21 April 2026 }}</ref> The lucifer sheartail is now placed with the [[beautiful sheartail]] in the genus ''[[Calothorax]]'' that was introduced in 1840 by the English zoologist [[George Robert Gray]].<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=21 April 2026 }}</ref>

==Description==
The lucifer sheartail is a medium-sized, {{convert|10|cm|adj=on}} long, green hummingbird with a long curved bill, small wings, and white streak behind its eye.<ref name="cornell">{{Cite web|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Lucifer_Hummingbird/overview|title=Lucifer hummingbird overview|publisher=All About Birds, Cornell University Laboratory of Ornithology|work=www.allaboutbirds.org|date=2019|access-date=2020-04-13}}</ref> The male has an [[Iridescence|iridescent]] [[plumage]], forked dark tail, green crown, long magenta gorget, and white underparts.<ref name=cornell/><ref name="vireo">{{Cite web|url=http://vireo.acnatsci.org/search.html?Form=Search&SEARCHBY=Common&KEYWORDS=lucifer+hummingbird&showwhat=images&AGE=All&SEX=All&ACT=All&Search=Search&VIEW=All&ORIENTATION=All&RESULTS=2|title=Lucifer hummingbird|date=2015|publisher=Visual resources for ornithology, The Academy of Natural Sciences of Drexel University|access-date=2020-04-13}}</ref> The female is larger with duller plumage, pale throat and white or buff feathers underside, usually with crimson trim.<ref name=cornell/><ref name=vireo/>

==Distribution==
The lucifer sheartail is distributed to [[desert]]s and [[arid]] areas with [[agave]] plants in the southwestern United States, from southwest [[Texas]], extreme southwestern [[New Mexico]] to southeastern [[Arizona]], and in central and northern Mexico.<ref name=cornell/> It is also found in the [[Madrean sky islands]] of the northern end of the [[Sierra Madre Occidental]], Mexico.{{cn|date=April 2020}} Its preferred habitat tends to be at altitudes of {{convert|3500|–|5500|ft}} in canyons, mountain slopes, and dry washes having desert shrubs and [[cactus|cacti]].<ref name=cornell/> In winter, the birds migrate to central Mexico.<ref name=cornell/>

==Diet==
The diet consists mainly of [[nectar]] from agave and colorful desert flowers, spiders and small [[insect]]s.<ref name=cornell/> Lucifer sheartails have a typical hummingbird flight style while feeding from flowers, catching insects in flight, and flying in straight lines to specific destinations for other food, the nest or for [[roosting]].<ref name=cornell/> Males defend feeding areas from males, other females, and [[black-chinned hummingbird]]s.<ref name=cornell/> However, most ''C. lucifer'' are non-territorial and forage at undefended agave or agave surrounded by dense vegetation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kuban |first1=Joseph F. |last2=Lawley |first2=John |last3=Neill |first3=Robert L. |date=1983-05-20 |title=The Partitioning of Flowering Century Plants by Black-Chinned and Lucifer Hummingbirds |journal=The Southwestern Naturalist |volume=28 |issue=2 |page=143 |doi=10.2307/3671382|jstor=3671382 }}</ref><gallery mode="packed" heights="140">
File:Lucifer Hummingbird (female) Guindani Trail Karchner Caverns SP AZ 2019-08-02 08-02-11 (48440340592).jpg|Female feeding
File:Lucifer Hummingbird, San Sebastián Atlahapa, Tlax., México imported from iNaturalist photo 77717019.jpg|Male feeding on [[Bouvardia ternifolia|firecrackerbush]]
</gallery>

==Breeding and behavior==
During [[courtship display|courtship]], males attract females by hovering high above the female, then dive with the wings or tail making a snap sound, then flying away with the tail feathers forked and making a different series of snapping sounds.<ref name=cornell/> The display lasts 30 to 45 seconds and may repeat several times an hour.<ref name=cornell/>

Females build nests on desert shrubs or cacti on steep, dry, rocky slopes, typically {{convert|2|-|10|ft}} above ground, sometimes on top of a previous nest.<ref name=cornell/> 

The female lays two white eggs in the small cup-like nest, having one or two [[brooding|brood]]s per season.<ref name=cornell/> The [[egg incubation]] duration is about 15 days, and the chicks nest for about 23 days.<ref name=cornell/> 

==Status==
A locally common species in its range, the lucifer sheartail is evaluated as stable and Least Concern on the [[IUCN Red List]] of Threatened Species.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/lucifer-hummingbird-calothorax-lucifer|title=Lucifer hummingbird, Calothorax lucifer|last=|first=|date=2020|website=Data Zone, Bird Life International}}</ref>

zh.extract:
瑰丽蜂鸟（台湾作傲麗蜂鳥，学名：Calothorax lucifer），是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
瑰丽蜂鸟体重约3.3克，翼长约39.3毫米，嘴峰长约23.5毫米，喙宽度约1.6毫米，喙厚度约1.6毫米，跗蹠长约4.1毫米，尾长约27.2毫米。瑰丽蜂鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的灌木丛中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。
瑰丽蜂鸟分布于美国西南部和墨西哥北部的干旱山区；越冬于墨西哥南部。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。
